retrieved_docs.title: Nicotinic Ganglionic Blocker (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine (ACh) is a neurotransmitter released from cholinergic neurons which binds two types of receptors: muscarinic and nicotinic ACh receptors (mAChRs and nAChRs, respectively). [5] Nicotinic ganglion receptors found in the autonomic ganglia are involved in both sympathetic and parasympathetic transmission. These receptors classify as neuronal nicotinic (Nn) cholinergic receptors, and they are on postganglionic efferent neurons. [1] Nn receptors are Cys-loop ligand-gated ion channels that, upon binding ACh, undergo a conformational change and become permeable to ions such as Na+, K+, and Ca2+, resulting in signal propagation through the postganglionic efferent neuron. [5] Nicotinic ganglion blockers exhibit a noncompetitive antagonism of Nn receptors, which results in blockade of signal transduction. [2] The blockade of Nn receptors at the level of the autonomic ganglia results in decreased sympathetic output, which reduces blood pressure, thereby producing the therapeutic effect. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholinergic Receptors -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The two receptors differ in function as ionotropic ligand-gated and G-protein coupled receptors, respectively. Nicotinic receptors function within the central nervous system and at the neuromuscular junction. While muscarinic receptors function in both the peripheral and central nervous systems, mediating innervation to visceral organs. The difference in signal transduction of the two receptor types confers separate physiological functions upon receptor activation. Furthermore, differences in receptor subtypes create unique implications for pharmacologic targets and pathogenesis of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Cholinergic Crisis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Nicotinic receptors, which are part of the ligand-gated ion receptor family, are activated by both ACh and nicotine. These receptors are located in muscle fibers at neuromuscular junctions and in autonomic ganglia, influencing both the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems. Excessive stimulation of nicotinic receptors at the skeletal muscle endplate and synaptic ganglia leads to several effects, including voluntary muscle fasciculation, which can progress to flaccid paralysis. In the cardiovascular system, this overstimulation initially causes tachycardia, which may eventually shift to bradycardia due to the opposing actions of muscarinic and nicotinic receptor activation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholinergic Receptors -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cholinergic receptors function in signal transduction of the somatic and autonomic nervous systems. The receptors are named because they become activated by the ligand acetylcholine. These receptors subdivide into nicotinic and muscarinic receptors, which are named secondary to separate activating ligands that contributed to their study. Nicotinic receptors are responsive to the agonist nicotine, while muscarinic receptors are responsive to muscarine.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Transmission -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Interestingly, the presynaptic part of the NMJ, the nerve terminal, also contains nicotinic ACh receptors. These receptors detect ACh in the synaptic cleft and regulate ACh release through a feedback system. When the concentration of ACh in the synaptic cleft increases sufficiently, the presynaptic ACh receptors sense this change, leading the nerve terminal to reduce further release of ACh. The key difference between the presynaptic and postsynaptic ACh receptors lies in their response to various ACh receptor agonists and antagonists. [5] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholinergic Receptors -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The autonomic nervous system is responsible for maintaining the homeostatic environment of the body with adjustments affecting major organ systems such as neuronal, circulatory, respiratory, integumentary, digestive, and urinary. The autonomic nervous system divides into sympathetic, parasympathetic, and enteric divisions. [9] Within the parasympathetic and sympathetic nervous system, neurons are categorized as preganglionic and postganglionic, depending on the location of their cell bodies within the central or peripheral nervous systems. The N2 or neuronal nicotinic receptor subtype exists on all postganglionic cell bodies. The N2 receptors are responsive to acetylcholine and transmit signals from the preganglionic to the postganglionic cell. [9] The ionic flux generated at the postganglionic cell is responsible for excitatory signal transduction to effector organs of the autonomic nervous system. Separately, the N1 or muscle nicotinic receptor is at the neuromuscular junction on muscle cells generating voluntary muscle movement. [10] An excitatory signal may become generated through N1 receptor activation. Depending on the strength of the signal, receptor activation may result in membrane depolarization with subsequent muscle contraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholinergic Receptors -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Cholinergic receptors perform major roles in neural transmission within the somatic and autonomic nervous systems. The nicotinic receptor subdivides into two subtypes, N1 and N2. N1 may also be referred to as the peripheral or muscle receptor type, while N2 is known as the central or neuronal receptor subtype. [3] The designation of the two receptors is primarily due to their distinctive locations within the autonomic and somatic nervous systems. The N1 receptor is present on skeletal muscle at the neuromuscular junction. N2 is within the peripheral and central nervous systems. N2 receptors are on the cell bodies of postganglionic neurons within the parasympathetic and sympathetic nervous systems. They are also on the adrenal medulla as a component of the sympathetic nervous system. The distribution of nicotinic receptors differs from that of muscarinic receptors, which primarily function within the autonomic nervous system, mediating the function of the parasympathetic subdivision.

retrieved_docs.title: Nicotinic Ganglionic Blocker (Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Nicotinic ganglion receptors found in the autonomic ganglia are involved in both sympathetic and parasympathetic transmission. These receptors classify as neuronal nicotinic (Nn) cholinergic receptors, and they are on postganglionic efferent neurons. The activation of the Nn receptor is mediated by the release of acetylcholine from preganglionic efferent neurons. Interruption of acetylcholine-receptor binding at the level of the autonomic ganglia results in complete interruption of both sympathetic and parasympathetic signaling. [1] Mecamylamine is one of the first nicotinic ganglion blockers widely used as a therapeutic agent, and it was originally introduced as an antihypertensive medication. [2] Increased sympathetic activity results in vascular vasoconstriction, and subsequently elevated blood pressure, as a result of Alpha-1 receptor stimulation. Mecamylamine's ability to blockade sympathetic activity at the level of the autonomic ganglia explains its long-time usage as an antihypertensive agent. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents -- Mechanism of Action -- Presynaptic Nerve Terminal
retrieved_docs.content: The presynaptic nerve terminal consists of motor neurons originating from the ventral horn of the spinal cord. The motor neuron loses its myelin sheath as it embeds in muscle tissue and secretes trophic and growth factors. The nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) are located on the surface of the presynaptic nerve terminal membrane. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: When an electric impulse transmits along the motor neuron, it causes the release of acetylcholine (ACh) from the presynaptic membrane, which travels across the synaptic cleft and acts on nicotinic receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, causing muscle contraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Anticholinergic Medications -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergic medications are competitive antagonists of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine at receptor sites within the cholinergic system. The cholinergic system utilizes two types of receptors, the plasma membrane-bound G protein-coupled muscarinic receptors, and the ligand-gated ion channel nicotinic receptors. [11] Nicotinic receptors are found in the postganglionic dendrites and nerve bodies of the autonomic nervous system and on the motor endplate of the neuromuscular junction. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetic Antimuscarinics -- Mechanism of Action -- Acetylcholine Receptors
retrieved_docs.content: The nervous system utilizes many neurotransmitters for communication and function. The primary neurotransmitter of the parasympathetic nervous system is acetylcholine (ACh). ACh is synthesized in the cytoplasm of presynaptic neurons by the enzyme choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) and is subsequently relocated to synaptic vesicles by vesicular acetylcholine transporter (VAChT), where it is stored. When the presynaptic neuron becomes stimulated via depolarization, ACh is released through exocytosis into the synaptic cleft, which acts on presynaptic and postsynaptic receptors by binding to them. These receptors include muscarinic (M) and nicotinic (N) types, each with their respective subtypes. There are 5 subtypes of muscarinic receptors, M1 through M5, all of which are G protein-coupled. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: NMBAs act at the neuromuscular junction (NMJ), which consists of 3 parts: Presynaptic nerve terminal Synaptic cleft Postsynaptic nicotinic receptors

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholinergic Receptors -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The neuromuscular junction works to convey the electrical signal of voluntary movement to mechanical action. The dysfunctional states of myasthenia gravis and congenital myasthenia syndromes disrupt transmission at the neuromuscular junction. Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disease that generates antibodies against the nicotinic receptor at the neuromuscular junction. The binding of pathologic antibodies to the receptor results in its loss of function and recycling back within the cell. [15] The loss of receptors at the cell surface results in fewer receptors being able to respond to a chemical stimulus and generate an appropriate electrical stimulus at the surface of the muscle cell. Congenital myasthenic syndromes are similar; however, they are not a result of immune dysfunction. There is most often a gene mutation in congenital syndromes, resulting in a dysfunctional nicotinic acetylcholine receptor. [16] Similarly, due to the loss or decreased function of the receptor at the membrane, the ability to generate the appropriate electrical impulse is impaired. Both mechanisms result in muscle weakness and fatigue, as the ability to produce the excitatory stimulus at the neuromuscular junction decreases.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Acetylcholine -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: There are two subtypes of nicotinic receptors, the muscular type (N1) and the neuronal type (N2). The muscular type is found specifically on the surface of muscle cells at the neuromuscular junction. The neuronal subtype is in the peripheral and central nervous systems. Specifically, N2 receptors are present in the adrenal medulla, on the postsynaptic cell bodies of neurons within the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems, as well as in various locations in the brain such as the ventral tegmental area, hippocampus, prefrontal cortex, amygdala, and the nucleus accumbens. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1714
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympathomimetic agent, meaning it mimics the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system by enhancing cholinergic neurotransmission. As a member of the parasympathomimetic class, galantamin exerts its therapeutic effects primarily through reversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase and allosteric modulation of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, actions that support cognitive function in conditions such as Alzheimer disease. Recognizing galantamin’s placement within the parasympathomimetic category helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, potential side‑effect profile, and appropriate use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1374
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and bethanechol are both classified as parasympathomimetics, a group of agents that mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. By stimulating cholinergic pathways, these drugs enhance parasympathetic tone, which can be therapeutically useful in conditions where increased muscarinic activity is desired. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action despite being used for different clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine is recognized as having a broader relationship with neurotransmitery, indicating that it functions as a neurotransmitter within the nervous system. This classification underscores acetylcholine’s role in transmitting signals between nerve cells and across neuromuscular junctions, which is fundamental to processes such as muscle contraction, autonomic nervous system regulation, and cognitive functions. Understanding acetylcholine’s position as a neurotransmitter is essential for comprehending its impact on both normal physiological signaling and various clinical conditions where its pathways may be altered.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1634
retrieved_docs.content: Tropatepine possesses a muscarinic receptor antagonist disposition, indicating that it functions as an antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: Adenosine and acetylcholine are both classified as neurotransmitters, meaning they serve as chemical messengers that transmit signals across synapses in the nervous system. As neurotransmitters, each plays distinct but complementary roles in modulating neuronal activity, influencing processes such as sleep regulation, cardiovascular function, muscle contraction, and cognitive function. Recognizing that adenosine and acetylcholine share this broader classification helps clinicians understand their contributions to normal physiology and to various disorders, guiding therapeutic strategies that target neurotransmitter pathways for conditions like arrhythmias, neurodegenerative diseases, and sleep disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1347
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine is classified as a neurotransmitter, reflecting its fundamental role in chemical signaling within the nervous system. Acetylcholine chloride, a salt form of acetylcholine, represents a more specific, narrower entity derived from acetylcholine and therefore falls under the broader category of neurotransmitters.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: inhibiting transmission at autonomic ganglia. The fixed‑dose combination of chlordiazepoxide and clidinium functions as a cholinergic antagonist, interfering with acetylcholine‑mediated signaling. Parasympatholytic agents are linked to anti‑emetic therapy because inhibition of parasympathetic activity helps prevent nausea and vomiting. The combination product chlordiazepoxide / clidinium is related to chlordiazepoxide hydrochloride, reflecting its inclusion of the hydrochloride salt. Collectively, these entities illustrate how individual drugs—such as aclidinium bromide for COPD, dihydroergotamine for adrenergic antagonism, the diverse anticholinergic and antitussive agents, and the wide array of compounds used for functional gastrointestinal disorders—fit into larger pharmacological families. Shared mechanisms (primarily receptor antagonism), hierarchical classifications, therapeutic roles, and safety considerations inform patient management across respiratory, gastrointestinal,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists, whose mechanism of action involves blocking β‑adrenergic receptors, have a contraindicated mechanism of action with methacholin, a muscarinic receptor agonist. Because the pharmacologic effects of β‑blockade oppose the cholinergic stimulation produced by methacholin—potentially leading to adverse outcomes such as enhanced bronchoconstriction or undesirable cardiovascular effects—these agents should not be used together in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 99
retrieved_docs.content: and lobelin is categorized as a ganglion‑blocking agent (gangliové blokátory). The hierarchical structure thus connects anticholinergic agents, adrenergic antagonists, antitussives, opioid analgesics, peptide‑hormone receptor antagonists, and related subclasses, clarifying mechanisms of action (acetylcholine or catecholamine blockade, peptide‑hormone receptor inhibition), therapeutic uses (bronchodilation, parasympathetic inhibition, cough suppression, cardiovascular modulation, analgesia), and safety considerations essential for clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 44
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergic agents are a pharmacologic subclass of Drugs and Biologicals that act as neurotransmitter antagonists through a receptor‑antagonist mechanism. The class comprises systemic antimuscarinic compounds such as homatropine, homatropine hydrobromide, homatropine methylbromide, profenamine, propantelin, glykopyrrolát, gangliové blokátory, and troxypyrrolium tosylate, as well as inhaled bronchodilators for obstructive airway diseases, exemplified by Aclidinium bromide used in COPD. Additional members include chlophedianol, chlophedianol hydrochloride, pirenzepin and Caramiphen hydrochloride (the latter also classified as an antitussive). Anticholinergics are positioned as an inverse subclass of substances characterized by receptor‑antagonist activity and are inversely related to antiparkinsonika, reflecting their relevance in movement‑disorder therapy. They are linked to autonomní látky and to the neuropeptide galanin, indicating shared autonomic pathways. As parasympatholytics

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2827
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamine and ambenonium chloride are both classified as parasympathomimetic agents, meaning they mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system by stimulating cholinergic receptors and enhancing parasympathetic tone. These drugs are used in clinical contexts where increased cholinergic activity is beneficial, such as in the treatment of certain neurological or autonomic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 159
retrieved_docs.content: and adrenergic agents are organized by pharmacologic action (muscarinic receptor antagonism, β‑adrenergic stimulation), chemical structure (quaternary ammonium, tropane, benzoic‑derived), and clinical indication (respiratory disease, functional gastrointestinal disorders, urinary frequency and incontinence), thereby supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and edrofonium are both classified as parasympathomimetic agents, meaning they mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. As members of this pharmacologic class, they enhance cholinergic transmission and can be used to support functions mediated by acetylcholine, such as cognition, memory, and certain autonomic processes. Recognizing both galantamin and edrofonium as parasympathomimetics helps clinicians understand their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications in conditions where augmentation of parasympathetic activity is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 32
retrieved_docs.content: network delineates a hierarchical drug classification—from broad receptor‑antagonist categories to specific anticholinergic, antitussive, myorelaxant, neuromuscular and antispasmodic subclasses—while emphasizing therapeutic applications (respiratory disease, pupil dilation, cough suppression, smooth‑muscle relaxation, muscle relaxation) and key pharmacologic interactions and contraindications essential for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1331
retrieved_docs.content: The PsycINFO subcluster term “Neurotransmitters & Neuroregulators (PsycINFO Subcluster Term)” includes acetylcholine as a member, indicating that acetylcholine is classified within this group of signaling molecules. Acetylcholine also has a broader relationship with neurotransmitery, reflecting its role as a neurotransmitter that participates in neural communication and regulation. Together, these relationships underscore acetylcholine’s placement in the larger category of neurotransmitters and neuroregulators, which is important for organizing and understanding its functions in psychological and neuroscientific research.

Anxiolytics may require co‑administration considerations with β‑blocking agents. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how β‑blockers, vasodilators, platelet‑aggregation inhibitors, enzyme replacements, statins, nicotinic‑acid derivatives and antiglaucoma agents intersect through shared mechanisms of sympathetic inhibition, vascular relaxation, platelet aggregation inhibition, enzyme substitution or lipid modification, guiding integrated therapeutic strategies across cardiovascular, metabolic, ophthalmic and rare‑enzyme disorders.
